headline: Chinese Trial Reported in Deer Lodge, 1873
body: The Montana sun, a brutal overseer, beat down on Bear Gulch in that August of 1873. Gold, the siren song of the West, had drawn men from every corner of the earth, and among them, the Chinese, to toil in the unforgiving earth. It was a life of raw struggle, where fortunes were made and lost with the swing of a pickaxe, and life itself was often cheap. In this hardscrabble existence, a man named Ah Wah met his end, not by the caprice of the mountain, but by the hand of another. Hang La Chung, known also as Ah Chung, stood accused. The details, stark and brutal, were laid bare. Ah Wah, shot by Ah Chung, the ball tearing into his flesh, four inches below the left nipple, exiting on the right side. A mortal wound, the lifeblood spurting forth in a frightful manner. Restoratives were offered, a futile gesture against the inevitable. But Ah Wah, with a gesture of his hand and a shake of his head, refused them. His Josh, he believed, had called him. And in a few short minutes, he bled out, his breath rattling to a halt in the dust of the gulch. The law, slow but inexorable, began its grind. Judge Woodlock, a man accustomed to the grim realities of the frontier, held an inquest. The jury, faced with the stark facts, rendered its verdict. The Deputy Sheriff, a figure of authority in a land where order was often tenuous, set out in pursuit of Ah Chung. He was a man, the newspaper reported, with a "bad reputation and character, both among Chinamen and the citizens of the community." Such was the judgment of the rough-hewn society that was forming in the territory. By December 6, 1873, the wheels of justice had turned to Deer Lodge. The "New North-West" newspaper, a voice of the burgeoning territory, carried the report: "The case of Hang La Chung, indicted as (Ah Chung) for the murder of Ah Wah, in Bear gulch last August, came up before the District Court on Wednesday." The selection of a jury was a protracted affair, several panels exhausted before twelve men were finally seated. The trial, a grim spectacle of frontier justice, began on Thursday evening. The verdict, when it came, was not for murder, but for manslaughter. A life taken, a life spared from the gallows, but bound for the confines of a territorial prison. The mining camps of Montana were a crucible, forging a new society from disparate elements, often with violence and prejudice as constant companions. The Chinese, industrious and resilient, faced a daily struggle against both the harsh land and the hostility of many who saw them as a threat. Yet, even in this raw environment, the machinery of the law, however imperfect, sought to impose its order. The trial of Ah Chung was a stark reminder of the thin veneer of civilization that covered the wild heart of the West, and the constant struggle for justice in a land where every man fought for his own claim, be it of gold or of life itself. He was found guilty of manslaughter, and would face his sentence on the morrow.
